sections.paragraph:
fut. יִשׁפֹךְ-

(1) to pour, to pour out (Arabic سفك id., Ethiop. ሠበከ፡ to cast metals, kindred to סָפַח, سفح), e.g. a drink-offering, Isa 57:6. שָׁפַךְ דָּם to pour out blood, i.e. to commit slaughter, Gen 9:6 37:22 Eze 14:19. Metaph. שָׁפַךְ נַפְשֹׁו Psa 42:5 and שׁ׳ לִבֹּו Lam 2:19 to Pour out one’s soul, i.e. to be poured out in tears and complaints, followed by לִפְנֵי יְיָ 1Sa 1:15 Psa 62:9 compare Lam. loc. cit., שָׁפַךְ חֲמָתֹו עַל to pour one’s anger upon any one, Eze 14:19 22:22 Lam 2:4.

(2) to heap up a mound (auffchütten), Eze 26:8.

Niphal-

(1) to be poured out, 1Ki 13:5. Metaph., Psa 22:15, “I am poured out like water,” a description of a man who cannot arise from weakness [Christ bearing our sins vicariously].

(2) to be poured out, i.e. profusely expended (as money), Eze 16:36 comp. ἐκχέω, Tob_4:18 .

Pual, to be poured out, used of one’s steps, i.e. to slip, Psa 73:2 קרי, compare the Lat. fundi, for prosterni.

Hithpael, to be poured out, Lam 4:1. The phrase, “my soul is poured out,” is-(a) it pours itself out in complaints, Job 30:16.-(b) my blood is poured out, I die, Lam 2:12. Hence-
